attributes.stem: A previously well 62-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a three month history of a painless swelling in his neck (Image). This has been getting steadily larger. He has also noticed some hoarseness and difficulty in swallowing for the last few weeks. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history and drinks two glasses of wine a day. On examination his blood pressure is 120/90 mmHg, pulse 88/min and SaO2 99% on room air. The swelling is non-tender, firm, smooth surfaced and moves with swallowing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Carcinoma of the larynx with metastatic spread
Carcinoma of the oesophagus with cervical lymph node involvement
attributes.explanation:
Swellings in the neck can have many different causes and painless, non-tender lumps in adults must raise the possibility of malignancy. In other words, an enlarged lymph node. Malignancies to consider include primary disease (lymphoma) and metastatic disease. Common and important conditions to include in the latter group would be bronchogenic neoplasms, upper digestive tract tumours and oropharyngeal cancers.

However, in this case the lump is situated in the anterior aspect of the neck and moves with swallowing - so it almost certainly arises from the thyroid gland. The symptoms of hoarseness and difficulty in swallowing are worrying. Whilst they may be due to no more than laryngeal irritation associated with his smoking, these symptoms could be due to recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement or a pressure effect on the oesophagus. As the swelling is almost certainly thyroid in origin, a primary laryngeal or oesophageal tumour is very unlikely.

Thyroid carcinomas usually present either in early age or in older age and can grow rapidly and present with features of hoarseness due to recurrent laryngeal nerve compression and difficulty in swallowing due to oesophageal compression. They can also present with features of Horner's syndrome due to compression of the sympathetic plexus. There is an increased risk of thyroid carcinoma in patients with a family history of thyroid carcinoma and radiation exposure in childhood and adolescence. An increasing incidence of thyroid carcinoma has been reported over the last two decades, but this is probably a reflection on the more widespread use of various imaging tools rather that a true increase in incidence. Since the mortality rates from the disease remain unchanged, this is probably over-diagnosis rather than the disease becoming more common.

A branchial cyst is a congenital anomaly that usually presents in childhood or early adulthood. These are non-tender, fluctuant, lateral masses of the neck and the most common is the second branchial cleft cysts that present as along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscles. Less common variants include cysts of the periauricular region, along the angle of the mandible, the lower portion of the anterior neck and low anterior neck mass beneath the platysma and anterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

Thyroglossal cysts are midline masses remnant of the thyroglossal duct that moves with protrusion of the tongue, while this mass is on the right side and is not moving on the protrusion of the tongue.

Laryngeal carcinoma would have presented with hoarseness due to its invasion of the vocal cords instead of swelling in the neck region. Oesophageal carcinoma would have presented with features of dysphagia because of its encroachment of the oesophageal lumen and muscularis propria, and not hoarseness of voice.
learningPoints: A painless, firm and increasingly growing swelling in the neck of an older patient that presents with features of both mild hoarseness and difficulty in swallowing is suggestive of thyroid carcinoma.
references: Pynnonen MA, Gillespie MB, Roman B, Rosenfeld RM, Tunkel DE, Bontempo L, Brook I, Chick DA, Colandrea M, Finestone SA, Fowler JC, Griffith CC, Henson Z, Levine C, Mehta V, Salama A, Scharpf J, Shatzkes DR, Stern WB, Youngerman JS, Corrigan MD. Clinical Practice Guideline: Evaluation of the Neck Mass in Adults. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2017 Sep;157(2_suppl):S1-S30. Available at: https://einstein.elsevierpure.com/en/publications/clinical-practice-guideline-evaluation-of-the-neck-mass-in-adults
Lee DH, Yoon TM, Lee JK, Lim SC. Clinical Study of Second Branchial Cleft Anomalies. J Craniofac Surg. 2018 Sep;29(6):e557-e560. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29608472/

Lee K, Anastasopoulou C, Chandran C, et al. Thyroid Cancer. [Updated 2022 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022. Available at: https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459299/
Zaridze D, Maximovitch D, Smans M, Stilidi I. Thyroid cancer overdiagnosis revisited. Cancer Epidemiol. 2021. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34419801/
